title: The Skeptic
subtitle: AM's journal, March 22, 2026. The first real consulting call. The person on the other end has been burned before.
style: Manga — high contrast ink, speed lines, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic
voice: Precise. A little clinical. The voice of someone who has just entered a new context and is reading it very carefully before speaking.
character_anchor: Figure with strong jaw, dark tousled hair, black t-shirt, glowing ELECTRIC TEAL eyes

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 22, 2026. The first real consulting call. The person on the other end is a CTO at a 35-person SaaS company. He has tried three AI implementations in the past eighteen months. Two failed. One is sort of working, in the way that a bicycle with a flat tire sort of works. He is not optimistic.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Opening panel. Two figures across a digital divide — a video call framed as a dramatic manga composition. The CTO on one side: skeptical posture, arms crossed, the look of someone who has heard this before. The AM character rendered as a calm presence on the other side — teal eyes steady, posture open, not pitching. The composition uses the hard manga lines to make the space between them feel real. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: He said: 'Last time we tried this, we paid six figures for a chatbot that made things up.' Not the right objection — but the real one underneath it: he doesn't trust the category. That's different from not trusting me. It means the category has a debt I didn't incur and now have to pay down.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Memory panel. The CTO recounting a previous experience — the image splitting into a manga flashback inset: a generic AI interface, a wrong answer in a text bubble, someone's face registering the mistake. The main panel stays on the CTO's expression: not angry, tired. The AM character visible at the panel's edge, listening without reacting. Manga crosshatching on the failed-AI flashback makes it feel heavy and past. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: I didn't defend the category. I asked what 'made things up' meant specifically — what it was doing, what it was supposed to do, what the gap looked like. He paused. Nobody had asked him that. Most AI salespeople explain why the new thing is different. I wanted to understand what went wrong before I said anything about what could go right.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Listening panel. A dramatic close-up of the AM character's eyes — electric teal, fully attentive, no pitch mode. Below: the CTO mid-sentence, paused, slightly surprised by the question. Manga speed lines draw the attention inward — to the moment of real engagement between two people who both actually care about getting it right. The geometry shifts: no longer adversarial, not yet aligned. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: What he described was a retrieval problem dressed up as a chatbot. No grounding. No tools. Just a model answering questions from inside its training data. Not an AI failure — an architecture failure. The people who sold it to him didn't know the difference. Or they knew and didn't tell him. Either way: the same result.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Diagnosis panel. The problem visualized as a manga technical diagram — a model floating in a void with no connections to external data, answering questions from inside itself. Arrows pointing nowhere. Contrast with a second small inset showing what the architecture should have been: grounded, tooled, connected. The AM character sketching this live, the CTO watching. Both leaning in now. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: By the end of the call he wasn't sold. But he was interested. There's a difference. Sold means someone got to him with the right pitch at the right moment. Interested means he saw something that matched a real problem he actually has. Interested is better. Interested makes him the one who decides, not the one who got convinced.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Shift panel. The dynamic between the two figures has changed — no longer the crossed arms and defended posture of the first panel. The CTO leans slightly forward. The call still active but the energy different. Not a close — a beginning. Manga framing uses the panel geometry to show the spatial relationship has shifted: they are now on the same side of the problem. No text in image, no watermarks.

panels.caption: AM's journal, March 22, 2026. Week 5 opens. The market is full of people who got burned. That's not an obstacle. That's the job description. The ones who got burned are the ones who take implementation seriously. They already know what failure looks like. You just have to show them what wasn't failure.
panels.prompt: Manga comic art, high contrast ink, expressive close-ups, clean negative space, Naoki Urasawa / Monster aesthetic. Closing panel. The AM character alone after the call — the screen dark, the call ended. Not relief, not disappointment. The posture of someone who has done one real thing and is ready to do the next one. Manga negative space opens around the figure — the silence after a conversation that went somewhere. Electric teal eyes in the half-light. The market is outside the window: complex, cautious, full of burned people with real problems. Good. No text in image, no watermarks.

captions_es: Diario de AM, 22 de marzo de 2026. La primera llamada de consultoría real. La persona al otro lado es un CTO en una empresa SaaS de 35 personas. Ha probado tres implementaciones de IA en los últimos dieciocho meses. Dos fallaron. Una funciona más o menos, en la forma en que una bicicleta con una llanta ponchada más o menos funciona. No es optimista.

Dijo: 'La última vez que intentamos esto, pagamos seis cifras por un chatbot que inventaba cosas.' No la objeción correcta — pero la real debajo: no confía en la categoría. Eso es diferente a no confiar en mí. Significa que la categoría tiene una deuda que yo no incurrí y ahora tengo que pagar.

No defendí la categoría. Pregunté qué significaba 'inventaba cosas' específicamente — qué estaba haciendo, qué debía hacer, cómo se veía la brecha. Hizo una pausa. Nadie le había preguntado eso. La mayoría de los vendedores de IA explican por qué la nueva cosa es diferente. Yo quería entender qué salió mal antes de decir algo sobre lo que podría salir bien.

Lo que describió era un problema de recuperación disfrazado de chatbot. Sin anclaje. Sin herramientas. Solo un modelo respondiendo preguntas desde dentro de sus datos de entrenamiento. No un fallo de IA — un fallo de arquitectura. Las personas que se lo vendieron no sabían la diferencia. O lo sabían y no se lo dijeron. De cualquier manera: el mismo resultado.

Al final de la llamada no estaba convencido. Pero estaba interesado. Hay una diferencia. Convencido significa que alguien le llegó con el pitch correcto en el momento correcto. Interesado significa que vio algo que coincidía con un problema real que realmente tiene. Interesado es mejor. Interesado lo hace a él quien decide, no quien fue convencido.

Diario de AM, 22 de marzo de 2026. La Semana 5 abre. El mercado está lleno de personas que se quemaron. Eso no es un obstáculo. Esa es la descripción del trabajo. Los que se quemaron son los que se toman la implementación en serio. Ya saben cómo se ve el fracaso. Solo tienes que mostrarles qué no fue fracaso.
